paper_metadata.title: Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws
paper_metadata.authors: Magnus Bergquist; Andreas Nilsson; Niklas Harring; Sverker C. Jagers
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: 33 countries (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania)
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: 89 eligible datasets from 51 articles; N=119,465 participants
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Meta-analysis of determinants of public acceptability/support toward climate-change taxes and laws, explicitly including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation.

determinants.definition: Perceived fairness of the climate change tax/law (fair treatment and fair cost/benefit distribution, e.g., by need or merit).
determinants.mechanism: Fairness perceptions increase perceived legitimacy and equitable burden-sharing, making people more willing to accept climate mitigation taxes/laws.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: fairness showed the strongest relationship with public opinion (r=0.65, 95% confidence interval (CI) (0.57, 0.71)).

determinants.definition: Belief that the climate change tax/law will fulfil its aim (especially mitigating climate change).
determinants.mechanism: Perceived effectiveness raises expected benefits and reduces skepticism, increasing acceptability of the policy.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: effectiveness to be the second strongest determinant (r=0.54, 95% CI (0.45, 0.63)).

determinants.definition: Degree of worry/concern about climate change.
determinants.mechanism: Greater concern signals higher perceived stakes, increasing motivation to support mitigation taxes/laws.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: Climate change concern was strongly related to public opinion (r=0.48, 95% CI (0.37, 0.58)).

determinants.definition: Perceived personal or societal threats to wellbeing associated with climate change.
determinants.mechanism: Higher perceived risk increases urgency and perceived need for mitigation, raising support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws

determinants.definition: Extent to which climate change is perceived as a serious problem.
determinants.mechanism: When climate change is seen as serious, mitigation policies appear more necessary, increasing acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws

determinants.definition: Belief that climate change is anthropogenic.
determinants.mechanism: Believing climate change is caused by humans strengthens causal understanding and supports mitigation taxes/laws.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: weak-to-medium-sized relationship between climate change belief and public opinion (r=0.23, 95% CI (0.14, 0.31)).
determinants.notes: Stronger correlation in North America (r=0.61) than Europe (r=0.17).

determinants.definition: Subjective or objective knowledge about climate change.
determinants.mechanism: More knowledge can improve understanding of risks/causes, modestly increasing policy acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: knowledge to be weakly positively related to public opinion (r=0.14, 95% CI (0.07, 0.20)).
determinants.notes: No significant difference: objective (r=0.16) vs subjective knowledge (r=0.13).

determinants.definition: Values prioritizing universalism/altruism and biospheric concerns (self-transcendent values).
determinants.mechanism: Pro-social/nature-oriented values align with climate mitigation goals, increasing support for taxes/laws.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: public opinion was positively related to self-transcendent values (r=0.26, 95% CI (0.21, 0.31)).
determinants.notes: Weaker in Asia (r=0.07) than Europe (r=0.28) and Oceania (r=0.30).

determinants.definition: Egoistic/self-enhancement values emphasizing authority, wealth, ambition and influence.
determinants.mechanism: Egocentric goal priorities can conflict with collective environmental sacrifices, lowering tax acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: public opinion was negatively related to self-enhancement values (r=ŌłÆ0.09, 95% CI (ŌłÆ0.12, ŌłÆ0.05)).

determinants.definition: Trust in institutions responsible for implementing climate mitigation policies.
determinants.mechanism: Trust reduces perceived vulnerability/mistrust, increasing willingness to accept policy burdens imposed by institutions.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: Results from our meta-analysis found a positive relationship for trust (r=0.23, 95% CI (0.19, 0.27)).
determinants.notes: Stronger for trust in implementing institutions (r=0.43) vs political institutions (r=0.21).

determinants.mechanism: Right/conservative identification is associated with lower acceptance of climate mitigation taxes, reflecting ideological conflict/polarization.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: identifying as politically right or conservative (versus left or liberal) was negatively associated with public opinion (r=ŌłÆ0.18, 95% CI (ŌłÆ0.24, ŌłÆ0.13)).
determinants.notes: Effect stronger in North America (r=ŌłÆ0.34) than Europe (r=ŌłÆ0.15) or Oceania (r=ŌłÆ0.09).

determinants.mechanism: Education may increase climate understanding and perceived legitimacy, modestly increasing tax/law support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: Education was positively associated with public opinion (r=0.13, 95% CI (0.11, 0.16)).

determinants.mechanism: Older people show slightly lower support/acceptance for climate mitigation taxes and laws.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: being younger (r=ŌĆō0.07, 95% CI (ŌĆō0.09, ŌĆō0.04)) was related to public opinion.
determinants.notes: Age effect significant for economic policies (r=ŌłÆ0.08) but not for regulations.

determinants.mechanism: Higher income is associated with slightly greater acceptability, potentially reflecting greater ability to absorb costs.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: having higher income (r=0.04, 95% CI (0.01, 0.07)) were related to public opinion.

determinants.mechanism: Meta-analytic overall effect is close to zero and not statistically significant.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Public opinion toward climate change taxes and laws (including tax instruments such as carbon taxes and fuel taxation, plus related legal/regulatory instruments) aimed at mitigating climate change.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public opinion/acceptability toward climate change taxes and laws
determinants.evidence_quote: Gender showed no significant overall effect (r=0.02, 95% CI (ŌłÆ0.01, 0.05)).
determinants.notes: Non-significant overall effect close to zero.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Determinants are estimated using a pooled outcome across ŌĆ£climate change taxes and laws,ŌĆØ not exclusively carbon/fuel taxes.
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: The paper includes market-based tax instruments and related legal/regulatory instruments; Table 1 pooled effects may partly reflect nonŌĆōtax-specific variance.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Reported effects are zero order correlations (determinants not jointly modeled).
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Authors note potential indirect pathways among determinants (e.g., trust via risk perception) but meta-analytic estimates are based on zero-order correlations.
